all_candidates.title: Wild sprint to finish line in Stage 6 of TDF
Tour de France: Fabio Aru soars to victory in Stage 5, Froome takes yellow jersey

transcript.clean_text: Marcel KD told us before the race the finish would be messy and would include fighting. He left out that a burst to the outside would lead to his win of stage six. You've seen this before. He won stage two this year. He knows this feeling very well. That's his 11th career stage win at the tour of France. Paul Vermeister alongside Bob Ro and Christian Vandervelt. Bob, it didn't look like he was in very good position late. How did he go from back in the pack to the winner? That's what makes him an incredibly successful sprinter. He's one of the most powerful riders in the professional pelaton. He makes such massive watts. If he has a is he has a straight shot to the finish line, very rarely does anybody come around him. And his timing is perfect. His instincts are great. The difference today, his team was much closer to the front throughout the final kilometers of the race. Yeah, they're much more proactive today. The other day on stage two or excuse me, with stage two, he he killed them. Stage four, way too far back. And then they got caught behind those crashes and just compounded farther and farther back, but half of his leadout train was on the ground. Um, today they're on the front, but then they actually got swamped and like you said, they're pretty far back with a couple hundred meters to go. you know, having that much HP underneath the hood, man. I mean, there's nobody like it, Marcel, in this kind of sprint. He's still my favorite for the green jersey. Yeah. How excited were the guys after the second longest trek to get to the finish and temperatures at 99° to make it to this point, but at the end, KD saw that opportunity on the outside. His last lead out man, Sabatini, pulling off on the fence there. And it doesn't look like he's helping him, but he is controlling the tempo in a way that suits Marcel KD. It's all strung out here. They're going nearly as fast as they will be going when they make that final burst to the to the line. You can see Demar in the green jersey pulling off just in front of KD. KD in the blue takes the inside line there. Pretty clever getting around riders. Now he just has a few riders in front of him. Couple hundred meters ago, he has them queued up in his sights and he decides now is the moment. slingshot off of Kristoff in the red with the white sleeves and no one can beat Marcel KD if he doesn't have to break if he's not impeded he's very rarely beaten in the sprint in the Tour to France number 11 and number two this year I think he has the hot hand amongst all the sprinters going to be very dangerous the rest of the Tour to France and Christian Bob talked about the watts and the power to go from eighth late to first as we're seeing here but also how about the vision that there was an opportunity on the outside and also the instincts on when it was time to go. Yeah, they're just sprinting. They didn't know exactly when they need to be going. They're looking at the signs once in a while. Probably scoped out the course beforehand on one of some kind of app or somebody even videotaped it ahead of the race. He knows exactly where he needs to be at every time. Um, and uh we could really bring ourselves into the front in the key moment and keep me there. So that was really important for the victory. Of course, the last kilometer um was then again a little bit freestyle, but I had a good wheel um first from Anuda and then I had to go at 250 to the front and uh yeah, it went perfect. I could start from a great position a little bit um behind to see where my uh concurrence go and yeah, I feel good at the moment. Indeed. And uh a second stage victory. Does that mean that you'll be focusing on the the green jersey now? Um, for sure I have it uh in my focus, but for now uh what is most important is that you get points at the at the in the sprints that you get those victories because they give you a lot of advantage. Dimension data was really big in that final especially when you saw Edel Balsson at the front really making you at first Quickstep had it all under control but then you saw the dimension data get to the front and really you know first of all that first roundabout with one kilometer to go and there's another one about 400 meters to go that really made things a little bit more chaotic but at the same time it almost made it safer because it stretched out that pelaton just that little bit if those corners weren't there it might have jumped up a or kind of bunched up just a little bit more and that's when we could see a a crash. But it's it's ironic to think that sometimes when there is twists and turns, it actually makes it safer for the sprinters because it actually elongates the pelon.
